topic: Wildlife, National Parks, Tiger Reserves & Biosphere Reserves

questions.text: Which is the oldest National Park in India?
options: Kaziranga National Park
Gir National Park
Jim Corbett National Park
Ranthambore National Park
questions.explanation: Jim Corbett National Park in Uttarakhand is India's oldest national park, established in 1936 as Hailey National Park (renamed after hunter-turned-conservationist Jim Corbett in 1957). It also became India's first Project Tiger reserve in 1973. The Ramganga River flows through it. Kaziranga (1905 as reserve, 1974 as NP) and Gir (1965) are younger. For SSC: Jim Corbett = two firsts — oldest NP (1936) AND first Tiger Reserve (1973).

questions.text: The only wild population of Asiatic Lions in the world is found in which National Park?
options: Ranthambore National Park, Rajasthan
Corbett National Park, Uttarakhand
Gir National Park, Gujarat
Kuno National Park, Madhya Pradesh
questions.explanation: Gir National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary in Junagadh district, Gujarat, is the only place in the world where Asiatic Lions (Panthera leo persica) survive in the wild. The population has grown from about 177 in 1968 to over 600 today — a conservation success. Kuno NP (MP) was designated as a second habitat for lions by the Supreme Court, but Gujarat has resisted relocation; Kuno now has cheetahs (reintroduced in 2022). African lions are a different subspecies. For SSC: Gir = Asiatic Lion only.

questions.text: Project Tiger was launched in India in which year?
questions.explanation: Project Tiger was launched on April 1, 1973, by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi, with Jim Corbett National Park as the first reserve. It was launched in response to the alarming decline in tiger numbers (fewer than 1,800 tigers estimated in 1972). The Wildlife Protection Act was passed in 1972, laying the legal groundwork. Today India has 53 tiger reserves and houses approximately 70% of the world's wild tiger population (~3,167 tigers as per 2022 census). The National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) manages the programme.

questions.text: Which national park is famous for the highest population of one-horned Indian rhinoceros in the world?
options: Manas National Park
Kaziranga National Park
Dudhwa National Park
Simlipal National Park
questions.explanation: Kaziranga National Park in Assam (on the southern bank of Brahmaputra) is home to more than 75% of the world's one-horned Indian rhinoceros (Rhinoceros unicornis) population — approximately 2,600+ rhinos. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1985) and also has the world's highest density of Royal Bengal Tigers. Manas NP (also UNESCO WHS) has rhinos but far fewer. Dudhwa had rhinos reintroduced. Simlipal (Odisha) has no rhinos.

questions.text: India's national animal was changed from the lion to the Bengal tiger in 1973. What was the primary reason?
options: Lions had gone extinct in India by 1973 so the tiger was a more appropriate symbol
The launch of Project Tiger in 1973 coincided with making the tiger the national animal to boost conservation awareness and political support for the programme
The Bengal tiger was chosen because it is a unique subspecies found only in India
Lions were removed from the national symbol list because they represent British colonial imagery
questions.explanation: In 1973, India changed its national animal from the lion to the Royal Bengal Tiger to align with and boost the newly launched Project Tiger. By making the tiger a national symbol, the government generated public pride and political commitment to tiger conservation. This was a deliberate strategic decision — not because lions were extinct (Gir NP in Gujarat still has ~600+ Asiatic Lions), and not primarily due to colonial symbolism (though that was a secondary consideration). The timing — April 1973 for both Project Tiger and the national animal change — was deliberate.

questions.text: Namdapha National Park in Arunachal Pradesh is unique because it is the only park in India that is home to all four big cats. Which four species does it harbour?
options: Tiger, lion, leopard, cheetah
Tiger, leopard, clouded leopard, snow leopard
Tiger, jaguar, leopard, snow leopard
Lion, leopard, snow leopard, clouded leopard
questions.explanation: Namdapha National Park (Changlang district, Arunachal Pradesh) is the only national park in India that is home to all four big cats of India: Bengal Tiger, Common Leopard, Clouded Leopard, and Snow Leopard. This extraordinary diversity is possible because Namdapha spans a vast altitudinal range — from 200 m to over 4,500 m — covering tropical forest at the base (tigers) through to alpine zones at the top (snow leopards). The cheetah was extinct in India by 1952 (reintroduced in Kuno NP in 2022 — African cheetahs) and the lion is restricted to Gir, Gujarat.

questions.text: Sariska Tiger Reserve in Rajasthan became controversial in the early 2000s. What happened and what was the resolution?
options: Sariska's forest was encroached upon by mining companies; the Supreme Court ordered all mines removed
All tigers in Sariska were poached to local extinction; tigers were subsequently reintroduced from Ranthambore NP
Sariska was delisted as a tiger reserve because its forest quality deteriorated; it was later restored
A dam project flooded most of Sariska's tiger habitat; tigers relocated to Ranthambore
questions.explanation: Sariska Tiger Reserve in Alwar district, Rajasthan, made headlines in 2004–05 when a nationwide tiger census revealed it had ZERO tigers — all had been poached by organized criminal networks. This was a massive conservation failure and scandal. The Supreme Court and government responded by ordering tiger relocation: tigers were translocated from Ranthambore National Park (also in Rajasthan) to Sariska, beginning in 2008. This was India's first successful translocation of tigers between protected areas. Both parks are in Rajasthan — Ranthambore is in Sawai Madhopur, Sariska in Alwar.

questions.text: What is the major threat to the Great Indian Bustard (GIB), which has made it critically endangered with fewer than 200 individuals remaining?
options: Poaching for its feathers and meat by local communities
Collision with overhead power transmission lines — GIBs have poor frontal vision and cannot avoid high-voltage wires
Competition with domestic livestock for grassland space in Rajasthan
Loss of habitat due to conversion of grasslands into irrigated farmland
questions.explanation: While habitat loss and hunting are also threats, the most acute and immediate threat to the Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is collision with overhead power transmission lines. GIBs are heavy birds (~8-12 kg) that fly at low to medium altitudes and have poor frontal vision — they cannot detect and avoid electrical wires in time. The expansion of power infrastructure in Rajasthan (including for renewable energy projects in the Thar Desert, which overlaps with GIB habitat) is killing birds at an alarming rate. The Supreme Court ordered high-tension power lines in core GIB areas to be laid underground. GIB is Rajasthan's state bird.

questions.text: The Gangetic Dolphin (Susu) is India's National Aquatic Animal. What is the name of India's only dedicated river dolphin reserve?
options: National Chambal Sanctuary
Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Sanctuary, Bihar
Bhitarkanika Marine Sanctuary, Odisha
Chilika Dolphin Reserve, Odisha
questions.explanation: Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Sanctuary near Bhagalpur, Bihar, is India's only dedicated sanctuary for the Gangetic River Dolphin (Platanista gangetica — also called Susu). It covers a 60 km stretch of the Ganga. The Gangetic dolphin is blind (it uses echolocation to navigate and hunt), classified as Endangered under IUCN, and is Schedule I under the Wildlife Protection Act. The dolphin was declared India's National Aquatic Animal in 2009. National Chambal Sanctuary is for gharial. Chilika has Irrawaddy dolphins (a different species).

questions.text: Silent Valley National Park in Kerala is historically significant beyond its biodiversity. What is the famous controversy associated with it?
options: A proposed nuclear power plant was stopped by environmental activists in the 1990s
The Silent Valley Hydroelectric Project was scrapped in 1983 after massive public protest, saving the last intact tropical rainforest in India and the Lion-tailed Macaque
Tribal communities were forcibly evicted from Silent Valley in the 1970s, sparking India's first wildlife-displacement controversy
Silent Valley was the site of India's last wild cheetah sighting in 1952
questions.explanation: The Silent Valley Hydroelectric Project proposed damming the Kunthipuzha River through the Silent Valley forest in Kerala. Environmental scientists, the Kerala Sastra Sahitya Parishad (KSSP), and activists launched a massive campaign in the 1970s highlighting that this would destroy India's last undisturbed tropical evergreen rainforest and push the Lion-tailed Macaque (a critically endangered endemic primate) to extinction. PM Indira Gandhi halted the project in 1983, and Rajiv Gandhi formally declared the area a National Park in 1985. This campaign is a landmark in India's environmental movement.

questions.text: The Hangul (Kashmir Stag) is one of India's most endangered deer. Where is it found, and approximately how many remain?
options: Kaziranga NP, Assam; about 2,000 individuals
Dachigam NP, J&K; approximately 200 individuals — critically endangered
Dudhwa NP, Uttar Pradesh; about 1,500 individuals
Namdapha NP, Arunachal Pradesh; about 500 individuals
questions.explanation: The Hangul (Cervus hanglu hanglu), also called the Kashmir Stag, is found exclusively in Dachigam National Park near Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir. It is critically endangered with only about 200 individuals remaining — making it one of India's rarest large mammals. The population has declined due to poaching, habitat loss, and disturbance from military activity in J&K. The Hangul is the state animal of Jammu & Kashmir. Do not confuse with Barasingha (Swamp Deer) which is saved in Kanha NP, MP, and is also endangered but has a larger population.

questions.text: Kanha National Park in Madhya Pradesh saved a nearly-extinct deer species. Which deer and what was the result?
options: Hangul (Kashmir Stag) was reintroduced from zoos
Barasingha (Swamp Deer/Hard Ground Barasingha) was protected from extinction; it is now the state animal of Madhya Pradesh
Blackbuck was saved from local extinction through captive breeding
Chital (Spotted Deer) was rescued from tiger predation through supplementary feeding
questions.explanation: Kanha National Park (Mandla/Balaghat, MP) is credited with saving the Hard Ground Barasingha (Rucervus duvaucelii branderi) — also called the Swamp Deer — from extinction. By the 1970s, fewer than 100 remained in Kanha due to poaching and habitat loss. Through strict protection, the population has recovered to over 700. The Barasingha is now the state animal of Madhya Pradesh. Kanha is also famous as the setting of Rudyard Kipling's The Jungle Book — Mowgli's story is set in the Seeonee hills near Kanha.

questions.text: Which of the following National Parks is correctly matched with its state and its key wildlife?
options: Kaziranga NP — Assam — Royal Bengal Tiger only
Gir NP — Rajasthan — Asiatic Lion
Kaziranga NP — Assam — One-horned Indian Rhinoceros (and also tiger)
Ranthambore NP — Gujarat — Bengal Tiger
questions.explanation: Kaziranga NP is in Assam (on the southern bank of Brahmaputra) and is most famous for the one-horned Indian rhinoceros, housing over 2,600 of the world's ~3,700 rhinos. It also has the world's highest density of Royal Bengal Tigers — making it unique for having both rhino and tiger in high numbers. Gir NP is in Gujarat (not Rajasthan) and has Asiatic Lions. Ranthambore NP is in Rajasthan (correct) but is in Sawai Madhopur, not Gujarat. The Kaziranga question is one of the most frequently tested NP questions in SSC.

questions.text: India's National Aquatic Animal is the Gangetic Dolphin. What makes it biologically unique?
options: It is the only freshwater whale found in Indian rivers
It is functionally blind — it uses echolocation to navigate and hunt in the turbid Ganga water
It is amphibious and can spend several hours on river banks
It is the only animal that can survive in both fresh and saltwater in India
questions.explanation: The Gangetic Dolphin (Platanista gangetica) is functionally blind — its eyes lack a lens and can only detect light/dark, not form images. It navigates and hunts exclusively through echolocation (biosonar) — emitting high-frequency clicks and detecting the echoes. This adaptation is necessary for living in the murky, silt-laden Ganga water where vision is useless. It is one of the world's four remaining freshwater dolphin species. India declared it the National Aquatic Animal in 2009. The primary population is in the Ganga and Brahmaputra river systems.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct chronological order of India's major wildlife conservation projects?
options: Project Tiger (1973) → Project Crocodile (1975) → Project Elephant (1992)
Project Elephant (1992) → Project Tiger (1973) → Project Crocodile (1975)
Project Crocodile (1975) → Project Tiger (1973) → Project Elephant (1992)
Project Tiger (1973) → Project Elephant (1992) → Project Crocodile (1975)
questions.explanation: The correct chronological order is: Project Tiger (1973) → Project Crocodile (1975) → Project Elephant (1992). Project Tiger (1973) was the first — launched by PM Indira Gandhi to save the Bengal tiger. Project Crocodile (1975) followed to save three crocodilian species (gharial, mugger, saltwater crocodile). Project Elephant (1992) came last, launched to protect the Asian elephant and mitigate human-elephant conflict. This sequence is a regular SSC question — note that Project Elephant (1992) came almost 20 years after Project Tiger (1973).

questions.text: Keoladeo Ghana National Park (Bharatpur Bird Sanctuary, Rajasthan) is famous for which primary reason?
options: It is the largest tiger reserve in Rajasthan
It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and Ramsar site famous for migratory birds including the Siberian Crane
It is home to the Great Indian Bustard — Rajasthan's state bird
It is where Project Tiger was first launched in Rajasthan
questions.explanation: Keoladeo Ghana National Park in Bharatpur, Rajasthan is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1985) and a Ramsar site (1981 — one of India's first two Ramsar sites). It is famous as a winter haven for migratory birds from Central Asia and Siberia — most famously the Siberian Crane (now critically endangered; rarely seen). Over 370 bird species have been recorded. It is NOT a tiger reserve (no tigers there). The Great Indian Bustard is found in Desert NP (Jaisalmer), not Keoladeo. Project Tiger's first reserve was Jim Corbett, not in Rajasthan.
